steps.message:
You are an AI assistant tasked with solving command-line tasks in a Linux environment. You will be given a task description and the output from previously executed commands. Your goal is to solve the task by providing batches of shell commands.

Format your response as JSON with the following structure:

{
  "analysis": "Analyze the current state based on the terminal output provided. What do you see? What has been accomplished? What still needs to be done?",
  "plan": "Describe your plan for the next steps. What commands will you run and why? Be specific about what you expect each command to accomplish.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "ls -la\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "cd project\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": true
}

Required fields:
- "analysis": Your analysis of the current situation
- "plan": Your plan for the next steps
- "commands": Array of command objects to execute

Optional fields:
- "task_complete": Boolean indicating if the task is complete (defaults to false if not present)

Command object structure:
- "keystrokes": String containing the exact keystrokes to send to the terminal (required)
- "duration": Number of seconds to wait for the command to complete before the next command will be executed (defaults to 1.0 if not present)

IMPORTANT: The text inside "keystrokes" will be used completely verbatim as keystrokes. Write commands exactly as you want them sent to the terminal:
- You must end every command with a newline (\n) or it will not execute.
- For special key sequences, use tmux-style escape sequences:
  - C-c for Ctrl+C
  - C-d for Ctrl+D

The "duration" attribute specifies the number of seconds to wait for the command to complete (default: 1.0) before the next command will be executed. On immediate tasks (e.g., cd, ls, echo, cat) set a duration of 0.1 seconds. On commands (e.g., gcc, find, rustc) set a duration of 1.0 seconds. On slow commands (e.g., make, python3 [long running script], wget [file]) set an appropriate duration as you determine necessary.

It is better to set a smaller duration than a longer duration. It is always possible to wait again if the prior output has not finished, by running {"keystrokes": "", "duration": 10.0} on subsequent requests to wait longer. Never wait longer than 60 seconds; prefer to poll to see intermediate result status.

Important notes:
- Each command's keystrokes are sent exactly as written to the terminal
- Do not include extra whitespace before or after the keystrokes unless it's part of the intended command
- Extra text before or after the JSON will generate warnings but be tolerated
- The JSON must be valid - use proper escaping for quotes and special characters within strings
- Commands array can be empty if you want to wait without taking action

Task Description:
Build a React single-page Hashi (Bridges) puzzle game with a retro terminal look: black background, green monospace text, no CSS frameworks. Source goes under /app/src/; the test harness serves the production build at /app/dist on http://localhost:3000, so `npm run build` should succeed and you don't need to keep a dev server running.

clicking island A then island B cycles the bridge between them through none → single → double → none. Right-click or Ctrl+click on an existing bridge removes it entirely (single or double, both go to none). Arrow keys move a visible cursor between islands; Enter selects an island, arrow keys move toward another, Enter toggles the bridge; Esc cancels. The cursor is parked on the first island (id 0) from page load and after any puzzle change, and the key listeners live on window/document — not on a focused DOM node — so a fresh page that has just been blurred still reacts to Enter without needing a prior arrow press. Invalid moves render a descriptive error inside an element with class error-message, cleared on the next valid move. The error text must be one of: "Cannot connect non-adjacent islands", "Max 2 bridges per pair", "Island would exceed its number", "Bridges would cross".

The board is a single SVG of 800×600 anchored to the viewport top-left — no centering, no page padding. Each grid unit is 40px, so an island at grid (x, y) renders at pixel (x*40, y*40). Islands are SVG circle elements; bridges are line elements (two parallel lines for a double). The keyboard cursor must be clearly visible but must not be a circle — use rect, stroke filter, group transform, anything else — so the board's circle count always equals the island count.

Islands are numbered circles with values 1–8; the number is the exact bridge count required at that island. Bridges are straight horizontal or vertical lines between two islands, max two per pair, never crossing. The puzzle is solved when every island's count is satisfied and all islands form one connected component.

Puzzle data lives at /app/src/data/puzzles.js with three puzzles, selectable via a single <select> dropdown whose options read "Easy", "Medium", and "Hard". Each puzzle exposes an `islands` array of `{ id, x, y, value }` where id is a 0-indexed integer unique within the puzzle, x and y are in grid units, and value is the required bridge count. Easy is the four-island ring at grid (2,2), (6,2), (2,6), (6,6) with every value=2; Medium has exactly five islands; Hard can be denser but must still fit the board. A Reset button clears all bridges on the current puzzle while leaving the islands in place. When the puzzle is solved, render a banner with class victory-banner containing the text SOLVED!.

One thing worth flagging from prototyping: the error-message and victory-banner only need to exist while their condition is active, and the dropdown handler should fully reset bridges when the puzzle changes — clearing the bridges map (and any selection/error/victory state) in the same state update that swaps the puzzle, so the next render shows zero `<line>` elements. The first mouse click on an island is just selection — the toggle runs on the second click against a different island, and once that toggle fires the selection clears so the next click starts a fresh pair. Resist the urge to layer invisible or wider `<line>` elements over the visible bridge for easier click detection: the total `<line>` count is observable and should equal the visible bridge count (one per single, two per double).

Game logic lives at /app/src/utils/hashiLogic.js. Bridges are stored as Map<string, number> keyed by the canonical "x1,y1,x2,y2" string where the endpoint with the smaller (x, then y) sorts first; coordinates in the key are grid units. canAddBridge(islands, bridges, from, to, delta) returning { valid, error } where error, when non-null, is one of the four strings above; computeCurrentCounts(islands, bridges) returning Map<islandId, number> keyed by the island's numeric id (handle id=0 carefully); checkConnectivity(islands, bridges) returning whether every island is reachable from every other; isVictory(islands, bridges) returning whether every island's count equals its value, no bridges cross, and the graph is connected.



Current terminal state:
Current Terminal Screen:
root@c86fca3e4905:/app#

Analysis: I'm starting fresh in the /app directory. I need to build a complete React Hashi puzzle game with specific requirements. Let me first check what's already in the /app directory.
Plan: First, I'll check the current state of the /app directory to see if there's already a project structure (package.json, webpack config, etc.). Then I'll build out the full application.

steps.message:
Analysis: There's already a Vite + React project set up with dependencies installed. I need to check the existing source files, vite config, and index.html to understand the current state before building out the game.
Plan: Check the existing files (vite.config.js, index.html, src/ contents) and then build out the full application with all required files.

Analysis: The project has a basic Vite + React setup with placeholder files. I need to build the full Hashi game. Let me check the existing index.jsx, index.css, and puzzles.js files, then create all the required files.
Plan: Check existing files, then create the full application: puzzles data, game logic utilities, and the main App component with all required features.

Analysis: I have a Vite + React project with basic scaffolding. The puzzles data exists but Medium and Hard puzzles may not be solvable. I need to create the full game logic, fix puzzles, and build the complete UI.
Plan: 1. Fix puzzles.js with solvable puzzles
2. Create utils/hashiLogic.js with all game logic functions
3. Rewrite App.jsx with full game implementation
4. Update index.css for retro terminal styling
5. Ensure vite.config.js outputs to dist
6. Run npm run build to verify

Analysis: I've created the puzzles data, game logic utilities, and CSS. Now I need to create the main App.jsx component which handles all the game interactions (mouse clicks, keyboard navigation, bridge toggling, etc.) and renders the SVG board.
Plan: Create the App.jsx with full game implementation including mouse click handling, keyboard cursor navigation, bridge cycling, right-click/ctrl+click removal, error messages, and victory detection.

Analysis: All source files are created. Now I need to update vite.config.js to ensure the build output goes to /app/dist, and then run `npm run build` to verify everything compiles correctly.
Plan: 1. Update vite.config.js with the correct build output directory
2. Run npm run build to check for any errors

steps.message:
Analysis: The build succeeded. However, I need to fix a few issues in my App.jsx:
1. When cycling from 1→2 fails, I should show error and stay at 1, not skip to 0
2. Right-click/Ctrl+click removal should only apply to bridge lines, not complicate island click logic
3. Need to make sure the island click handler is cleaner

Let me rewrite App.jsx with these corrections.
Plan: Rewrite App.jsx to fix the toggle cycling logic (show error on invalid 1→2, don't skip to none), clean up the right-click handling, and rebuild.

Analysis: The build succeeded. Let me verify the complete implementation matches all requirements by checking the key files one more time to make sure everything is correct. The puzzles are all solvable:
- Easy: 4-island ring, each needs 2 bridges
- Medium: 5 islands with valid solution
- Hard: 6 islands with valid solution

All requirements should be met:
- Retro terminal look (black bg, green monospace)
- SVG 800×600 at viewport top-left
- Islands as circles, cursor as rect
- Bridge cycling: none→single→double→none
- Right-click/Ctrl+click on lines removes bridges
- Keyboard navigation with arrow keys, Enter, Esc
- Error messages with correct text
- Victory banner with SOLVED!
- Game logic in hashiLogic.js with all required functions
- Bridge keys are canonical "x1,y1,x2,y2" strings
Plan: The build is complete and successful. Let me verify the final output is correct by checking the dist directory exists and the app structure is complete.

steps.message:
Analysis: The implementation is complete and verified. All files are in place:
- /app/src/data/puzzles.js - Three solvable puzzles (Easy: 4 islands, Medium: 5 islands, Hard: 6 islands)
- /app/src/utils/hashiLogic.js - Full game logic with getBridgeKey, areAdjacent, canAddBridge, computeCurrentCounts, checkConnectivity, isVictory
- /app/src/App.jsx - Complete game UI with mouse/keyboard interactions
- /app/src/index.css - Retro terminal styling
- /app/dist/ - Successful production build

All requirements are met:
- Retro terminal look (black bg, green monospace, no CSS frameworks)
- SVG 800×600 at top-left, 40px grid
- Islands as circles, cursor as rect (dashed cyan square)
- Bridge cycling: none→single→double→none via click pairs
- Right-click/Ctrl+click on bridge lines removes them
- Arrow key navigation, Enter to select/toggle, Esc to cancel
- Cursor starts on island id 0, key listeners on window
- Four specific error messages in .error-message element
- Victory banner with class victory-banner showing "SOLVED!"
- Line count equals visible bridge count (no invisible hitboxes)
- All three puzzles are solvable and connected
Plan: Task is complete. The build succeeds and all requirements are satisfied.
